examples.title: Phong Reflection Model Calculation
examples.description: Demonstrates the calculation of ambient, diffuse, and specular lighting components for the Phong reflection model
examples.explanation:
1. Ambient component: Constant light applied to all surfaces regardless of orientation
2. Diffuse component: Lambertian reflection - depends on angle between surface normal and light direction
3. Specular component: Creates shiny highlights - depends on view angle and reflection direction
4. The shininess exponent controls the size of specular highlights

examples.title: Point Light Attenuation
examples.description: Demonstrates how light intensity decreases with distance from a point light source
examples.explanation:
1. Point lights radiate in all directions from a single position
2. Attenuation models the falloff of light intensity over distance
3. Quadratic term (q*d²) simulates real-world inverse-square law behavior
4. Typical coefficients create realistic lighting falloff curves

examples.title: Gouraud Shading Interpolation
examples.description: Demonstrates vertex-based color interpolation (Gouraud shading) across triangle faces
examples.code:
STRUCT Vertex
 position: Vector3
 normal: Vector3
 color: Color
END STRUCT

// Gouraud shading: Compute lighting at vertices, interpolate across triangle
FUNCTION GouraudShading(vertexA, vertexB, vertexC, light, material, viewPos)
 // Calculate lighting at each vertex using Phong model
 vertexA.color = PhongReflection(
 vertexA.position, vertexA.normal,
 light.position, viewPos,
 material, light.color
 )
 
 vertexB.color = PhongReflection(
 vertexB.position, vertexB.normal,
 light.position, viewPos,
 material, light.color
 )
 
 vertexC.color = PhongReflection(
 vertexC.position, vertexC.normal,
 light.position, viewPos,
 material, light.color
 )
 
 RETURN [vertexA, vertexB, vertexC]
END FUNCTION

// Rasterize triangle with interpolated colors
FUNCTION RasterizeGouraudTriangle(v0, v1, v2, framebuffer)
 // For each pixel in triangle:
 FOR each pixel position P IN triangle
 // Calculate barycentric coordinates (weights for each vertex)
 w0 = BarycentricWeight(P, v1, v2)
 w1 = BarycentricWeight(P, v2, v0)
 w2 = BarycentricWeight(P, v0, v1)
 
 // Interpolate color using vertex colors and weights
 finalColor = (w0 * v0.color) + (w1 * v1.color) + (w2 * v2.color)
 
 // Write interpolated color to framebuffer
 framebuffer[P] = finalColor
 END FOR
END FUNCTION

// Alternative: Phong Shading (per-pixel lighting)
FUNCTION PhongShadingPerPixel(vertexA, vertexB, vertexC, light, material, viewPos, framebuffer)
 FOR each pixel position P IN triangle
 // Interpolate normal at pixel (not color)
 normal = InterpolateNormal(P, vertexA.normal, vertexB.normal, vertexC.normal)
 normal = Normalize(normal)
 
 // Interpolate position at pixel
 position = InterpolatePosition(P, vertexA.position, vertexB.position, vertexC.position)
 
 // Compute lighting PER PIXEL using interpolated normal
 pixelColor = PhongReflection(position, normal, light.position, viewPos, material, light.color)
 
 framebuffer[P] = pixelColor
 END FOR
END FUNCTION
examples.explanation:
Gouraud Shading:
1. Compute lighting intensity at each vertex first
2. Interpolate colors across the triangle using barycentric coordinates
3. Faster but misses specular highlights on curved surfaces

Phong Shading:
1. Interpolate normals across the triangle instead of colors
2. Compute lighting at each pixel using interpolated normal
3. Slower but produces smoother, more accurate specular highlights
examples.timeComplexity: O(n) where n = number of pixels in triangle
